text: Inter-Agency Reproductive Health Kits for Crisis Situations (6th edition)

The Inter-Agency Reproductive Health (IARH) Kits are pre-packaged sets of reproductive health supplies for use in humanitarian emergencies. They are designed to cover the most critical reproductive health needs during the acute phase of a crisis when normal supply chains are disrupted. Kits are numbered 0–10, each serving a distinct function and target facility or population.

Kits are quality-assured and ready to deploy. They are packed in standardized quantities to serve defined populations for defined time periods, making procurement and logistics planning more predictable. All kits in the 6th edition have been updated to reflect current clinical guidelines and procurement standards as of 2019.
Overview and Kit Selection Guidance

Kit selection depends on: (1) the target population size, (2) facility level (community, primary, referral), (3) phase of emergency (acute vs. stabilisation), and (4) specific service gaps identified in the initial RH assessment. Not all kits are needed in every context. The minimum set for MISP implementation is Kits 0, 1, 2, and 4.

Ordering calculation: For a displaced population of 10,000, standard monthly requirements are approximately: 10 × Kit 0, 2 × Kit 1, 2 × Kit 2, 1 × Kit 3 (per quarter), 1 × Kit 4 (replenish as used), 2 × Kit 5, 2 × Kit 6. These are minimum estimates — actual consumption should be tracked and orders adjusted monthly.
Kit 0: Clean Delivery Kit (Individual)

Kit 0 is a single-use kit for one delivery, designed for distribution to pregnant women for use if they cannot reach a health facility. It contains: 1 bar soap, 1 pair of gloves, 1 plastic sheet, 3 pieces of string or cord clamps, 1 razor blade, 1 receiving cloth for the newborn, and instructions. It does NOT provide sterile conditions — it reduces contamination risk when no other option is available.

Target population: All pregnant women who may not reach a health facility for delivery. Distribution should begin at the onset of emergency. Recommend distributing to all women in the third trimester as a priority. Include instructions in local language. Kit 0 is a last resort — facility delivery with a skilled birth attendant is always preferable.
Kit 1: Safe Delivery Kit (Health Post)

Kit 1 is designed for health facilities providing basic delivery care — health posts and primary health centres. It contains supplies for approximately 10 deliveries: gloves, cord clamps, syringes, suture material, oxytocin ampoules, IV fluids and giving sets, catheter, blood pressure cuff, and fetoscope. It enables active management of the third stage of labour (AMTSL) to prevent postpartum haemorrhage.

Kit 1 supports BEmOC (Basic Emergency Obstetric Care) signal functions at primary level. It does not include surgical supplies — for caesarean section, refer to a CEmOC facility. Oxytocin in Kit 1 must be stored in a cold chain (2–8°C); check cold chain availability before ordering.
Kit 2: Condom Supply Kit

Kit 2 contains 1,440 male condoms (12 gross) and 360 female condoms. Male condoms meet WHO/UNFPA quality standards. Female condoms are included to address situations where women cannot negotiate condom use with male partners. Condoms should be distributed free of charge and displayed prominently at health facilities, distribution centres, and community meeting points.

Communication is essential alongside distribution. Condom use messaging should be culturally appropriate and include information on dual protection (prevention of both STIs and unintended pregnancy). Peer education and community health worker distribution increase uptake. Do not store condoms in direct sunlight or at temperatures above 40°C — heat degrades latex.
Kit 3: STI/RTI Syndromic Treatment Kit

Kit 3 supplies are for syndromic management of sexually transmitted and reproductive tract infections (STIs/RTIs) at primary health facility level, for a population of 10,000 for 3 months. Contents include antibiotics for urethral discharge, vaginal discharge, genital ulcer, and pelvic inflammatory disease — in accordance with WHO syndromic management protocols. No laboratory tests are required.

Key protocols included: (1) Vaginal discharge syndrome — metronidazole + one of doxycycline or azithromycin, (2) Urethral discharge — cefixime + doxycycline or azithromycin, (3) Genital ulcer — benzathine penicillin + aciclovir + metronidazole (cover syphilis, HSV-2, and chancroid empirically), (4) PID — doxycycline + metronidazole ± ceftriaxone. Treat partners of all cases.
Kit 4: Post-Rape Treatment Kit

Kit 4 provides clinical care for one survivor of sexual violence. Each kit contains: HIV post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for 28 days, emergency contraception (levonorgestrel), antibiotics for STI prophylaxis (gonorrhoea, chlamydia, syphilis, and trichomoniasis), hepatitis B vaccine (first dose), and wound care supplies. Psychosocial first aid is not in the kit but must accompany clinical care.

Critical time thresholds: PEP must be started within 72 hours of exposure (ideally within 2 hours). Emergency contraception must be given within 120 hours (5 days) — earlier is more effective. Hepatitis B vaccination should be given as soon as possible. Document care on a standardized, confidential intake form. Do not share information without survivor consent.

Kit 4 should be stocked at every primary health facility. Staff must be trained in survivor-centred care before the kit is deployed. If trained staff are not available, a trained community health worker can provide first aid and immediate referral while arrangements are made. Never delay referral while waiting for trained staff.
Kit 5: Oral Contraceptive Kit

Kit 5 contains combined oral contraceptive pills (COCs) — 1,800 monthly cycles — for a population of 10,000 for 3 months. The kit uses WHO-recommended first-line formulations (low-dose oestrogen-progestogen combination). COCs are effective, reversible, and require no procedure. They can be provided by a trained health worker with a simple checklist to rule out contraindications.

Family planning services should be offered from the first days of an emergency. Unintended pregnancy in a humanitarian setting carries heightened risk — interrupted antenatal care, risk of unsafe abortion, nutrition challenges, and protection concerns. Women have the right to decide when and whether to become pregnant, regardless of their displacement status.
Kit 6: Injectable Contraceptive Kit

Kit 6 contains depot medroxyprogesterone acetate (DMPA) — 1,800 doses — for a population of 10,000 for 3 months. DMPA is given as an intramuscular injection every 3 months. It is highly effective, does not require daily pill-taking (important in chaotic conditions), and has no oestrogen-related contraindications. It is suitable for breastfeeding women. Pregnancy must be excluded before first injection.
Kit 7: IUD Kit

Kit 7 contains copper IUDs and insertion supplies — 100 IUDs per kit. The IUD is the most effective reversible contraceptive method. It can be inserted by a trained provider (midwife or doctor) at a primary facility or above. It is appropriate for post-partum insertion (within 48 hours of delivery or after 4 weeks). The IUD can also serve as emergency contraception if inserted within 5 days of unprotected sex.
Kit 8: Safe Abortion Kit

Kit 8 contains misoprostol and mifepristone for medical management of abortion and post-abortion care, in contexts where safe abortion is legally permitted. Contents include: misoprostol tablets, mifepristone tablets, manual vacuum aspiration (MVA) equipment, and post-abortion contraception supplies. This kit must only be used in settings where safe abortion is legally permitted and by trained providers.

Even where abortion is legally restricted, misoprostol and MVA equipment in Kit 8 are essential for management of complications of unsafe abortion and miscarriage — which are not restricted by law. Haemorrhage and sepsis from unsafe abortion are major causes of maternal death. Treatment of complications is always legal and required.
Kit 9: Antenatal Care Supplement Kit

Kit 9 contains nutritional supplements for antenatal care for a population of 10,000 for 3 months: iron-folic acid tablets, calcium supplementation, and vitamin A capsules. These supplements address the most common nutritional deficiencies in pregnancy in humanitarian settings. They should be provided at every ANC contact. Iron-folic acid reduces anaemia risk; calcium reduces pre-eclampsia risk in calcium-deficient populations.
Kit 10: Newborn Care Kit

Kit 10 contains supplies for immediate newborn care: cord clamps, 7.1% chlorhexidine gel for cord care, resuscitation equipment (bag-valve-mask in two sizes), suction bulb, and thermal care supplies (plastic wrapping for very low birth weight newborns). Chlorhexidine cord application reduces neonatal mortality from cord infection (omphalitis) by approximately 23% in community settings.

Neonatal resuscitation supplies in Kit 10 should be available at every birth. The bag-valve-mask must be correctly sized and staff must be trained. Key skills: drying and stimulating, positioning, bag-mask ventilation. Neonatal death from birth asphyxia is preventable with simple resuscitation in most cases.
Cold Chain Requirements

Several IARH Kit components require cold chain storage: oxytocin (Kit 1: 2–8°C, protected from light and freezing), hepatitis B vaccine (Kit 4: 2–8°C), and some antibiotics (check product-specific requirements). Before ordering, confirm that cold chain capacity exists at the receiving facility. Oxytocin that has been frozen or exposed to heat above 8°C is degraded and ineffective — it must be discarded.
Monitoring Kit Consumption

Track kit usage from day one. Record: number of kits received, distributed, and remaining; items used from opened kits; dates of distribution. Report consumption monthly to the lead RH agency and supply chain coordinator. Stockout is an emergency — if any MISP commodity is at risk of running out within 30 days, raise a supply alert immediately. Stockouts cost lives.
